content:
```json
[
  {
    "id": "f1",
    "front": "What is the primary motivation for using a Student's t-distribution instead of a Gaussian in a probabilistic model?",
    "back": "To introduce heavier tails, which provides more robustness to outliers. This is crucial when modeling real-world data that often contains anomalies or errors that a Gaussian would assign unrealistically low probability."
  },
  {
    "id": "f2",
    "front": "How is the Student's t-likelihood function defined for a regression problem?",
    "back": "It replaces the Gaussian noise model. For observed data $y_n$ given inputs $x_n$ and parameters $\\mathbf{w}$, it is: $p(y_n | x_n, \\mathbf{w}, \\sigma^2, \
u) = \\text{St}(y_n | \\mathbf{w}^\\top \\phi(x_n), \\sigma^2, \
u)$, where $\\phi$ is a basis function, $\\sigma^2$ is the scale parameter, and $\
u$ is the degrees of freedom controlling the tail heaviness."
  },
  {
    "id": "f3",
    "front": "What is the key mathematical relationship between the Student's t-distribution and a Gaussian scale mixture?",
    "back": "A Student's t-distribution can be expressed as an infinite mixture of Gaussians, where the precision (inverse variance) of each Gaussian is distributed according to a Gamma distribution: $\\text{St}(x | \\mu, \\sigma^2, \
u) = \\int \\mathcal{N}(x | \\mu, (\\lambda \\sigma^2)^{-1}) \\cdot \\text{Ga}(\\lambda | \
u/2, \
u/2)  d\\lambda$."
  },
  {
    "id": "f4",
    "front": "In the context of the EM algorithm for a model with a Student's t-likelihood, what is the role of the latent variable $\\lambda_n$?",
    "back": "Each data point $y_n$ has an associated latent precision variable $\\lambda_n$. In the E-step, we compute $\\mathbb{E}[\\lambda_n]$, which acts as a weight. Outliers will have a low expected $\\lambda_n$, effectively downweighting their contribution to the parameter estimates in the M-step, making the model more robust."
  },
  {
    "id": "f5",
    "front": "What is the effect of the degrees of freedom parameter ($\
u$) on the shape of the Student's t-distribution?",
    "back": "It controls the heaviness of the tails. As $\
u \\to \\infty$, the distribution converges to a Gaussian distribution with thin tails. As $\
u \\to 0$, the tails become infinitely heavy. A lower $\
u$ value provides greater robustness to outliers."
  }
]
```
